This image depicts a **grayscale axial magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan** of a human brain, captured from a top-down perspective. The scan highlights anatomical structures with high contrast, showcasing the cerebral hemispheres in varying shades of gray and white. The **lateral ventricles** are visible as symmetrical, fluid-filled cavities near the center, with the **interventricular septum** (a membrane separating them) appearing as a darker central structure. The **spectral cortex** (gray matter) and **white matter** are discernible, along with the **gyri** (cortical ridges) and **sulci** (deep grooves).  

The brain’s outer boundary is delineated by a darker, well-defined outline, consistent with cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) spaces.

OFFICIAL REPORT: Abnormal. A tumor is present.

Official Report. Dorsal surface of a human brain with a suspected glioma abnormality. Specifically, a focal hyperintense area in the right cerebellum adjacent to the fourth ventricle. The lesion has an irregular, heterogeneous structure due to partial necrosis, involving gray matter. The fourth ventricle is effaced, indicating mass effect from the tumor. Compressed cerebellum with no edema or peritumoral mass effect. Study conducted using a 3D volumetric acquired sequence, likely Fat SAT 3D Flip Angle Inversion MRI. Final diagnosis for this patient: Cerebellar glioma. WHO grade II.
